Details

Water at high pressure undergoes Joule–Thomson expansion when passing through a throttling valve. This process is assumed to be adiabatic, so the enthalpy change is zero.

\(\Delta H = 0\), so \(H_1 = H_2\), where \(H_1\) is the inlet enthalpy and \(H_2\) is the exit enthalpy.

When two phases are present, the saturated vapor enthalpy \(H^V\) and the saturated liquid enthalpy \(H^L\) are used to determine the fraction of the exit stream that is vapor. For the conditions chosen, liquid and vapor exit the throttle at saturation conditions, because \(H^L < H_2 < H^V\).

The steam quality \(q\) is calculated by:

\(H_2 = q H^V + (1 - q) H^L\),

so that \(q = \dfrac{H_2 - H^L}{H^V - H^L}\).

When only liquid is present \(q = 0\) and \(H_2 = H^L\).
